title: The Galton Board: Where Bell Curves Come From

md: Drop a single marble through a triangular field of pegs. At each peg, it bounces left or right with equal probability. After many pegs, it lands in one of the bins at the bottom — but *which* bin is anyone's guess.
Now drop a thousand marbles. The bins fill in a way that is *not* anyone's guess.
Try **Drop 1** a few times — you can predict almost nothing about where any single marble lands. Now **Drop 1000**. A bell-shaped histogram appears. **Order from chaos.**
What you're seeing has two layered names in mathematics:

blocks.title: Two famous theorems in one widget
blocks.body:
**Law of Large Numbers** — as the number of marbles grows, the histogram's *shape* settles down (each bin's fraction converges to a fixed probability). Random in the small; predictable in the large.

**Central Limit Theorem** — that limiting shape isn't arbitrary. The sum of many independent ± choices, each small, approaches a **normal distribution** (the bell curve). This is why bell curves appear everywhere in nature.

md: ### Why a bell, not (say) a square?

Each marble's bin number is the **count of right-bounces** out of $n$ pegs. To land in the leftmost bin (0 rights), you need ALL $n$ bounces to go left — probability $(1/2)^n$. To land in the rightmost bin (all rights), same tiny probability. But to land in the middle bin (about $n/2$ rights), there are $\binom{n}{n/2}$ different ways to do it — and that count is HUGE compared to the corners. **The middle bins are favored not because each marble aims there, but because there are many more paths leading there.**
Mathematically, $P(\text{bin } k) = \dbinom{n}{k}\,(1/2)^n$. This is the binomial distribution. As $n$ grows, it approaches the normal distribution centered at $n/2$.
### What the green dashed curve shows

Toggle the "show theoretical bell curve" checkbox. The dashed green line is what the bins *should* converge to — the binomial probabilities scaled to your total number of drops. After 10 drops the histogram looks ragged; after 100 it's mostly there; after 1000 your bars hug the curve almost perfectly. **That's the Law of Large Numbers, made visible.**

blocks.title: Why this matters
blocks.body:
Anywhere many small independent random effects add up, you get a bell curve:

- **Heights of people** — sum of many small genetic and environmental contributions.
- **Measurement errors** — many tiny independent error sources.
- **Test scores, IQ** — sum of many small ability contributions.
- **Asset returns** (approximately, with caveats) — sum of many small market influences.

The Galton board is the cleanest possible demonstration of *why* the normal distribution is the 'default' distribution in nature and science.

md: **Compare drops:** drop 10 marbles, then reset and drop 1000. Notice how the shape stabilizes only in the long run. Single drops are unpredictable; large samples are highly predictable. That's the whole point of statistics.

**Read the stats:** the header shows the running **mean** (where the marbles center) and the **standard deviation** $\sigma$ (how spread out they are). For a 12-row board, the mean settles around 6 (half the pegs went right on average) and $\sigma \approx \sqrt{n/4} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$. Compare to your widget's reading after 1000 drops.
